title: Loose equality (==)
body:
## Definition

**Loose equality (`==`)** compares values with [type coercion](concept:type-coercion) — `null == undefined` is true, but `null == 0` is false.

## Why it exists

Legacy JavaScript behavior before `===` was widely adopted.

## How it works

```js
0 == false; // true
"" == 0; // true
null == undefined; // true
[] == false; // true
```

Coercion rules follow abstract equality algorithm — often surprising.

## Common confusion

Almost always prefer [strict equality](concept:strict-equality) (`===`). Exception: `value == null` checks both `null` and [undefined](concept:undefined).

## In practice

Lint rules (`eqeqeq`) ban `==` in modern codebases except intentional null checks.
bodyLinked:
## Definition

**[Loose equality](concept:loose-equality) ([==](concept:loose-equality))** compares values with [type coercion](concept:type-coercion) — `null == undefined` is true, but `null == 0` is false.

## Why it exists

Legacy JavaScript behavior before [===](concept:strict-equality) was widely adopted.

## How it works

```js
0 == false; // true
"" == 0; // true
null == undefined; // true
[] == false; // true
```

Coercion rules follow [abstract equality](concept:loose-equality) algorithm — often surprising.

## Common confusion

Almost always prefer [strict equality](concept:strict-equality) ([===](concept:strict-equality)). Exception: `value == null` checks both [null](concept:null-value) and [undefined](concept:undefined).

## In practice

[Lint rules](concept:coding-standards) (`eqeqeq`) ban [==](concept:loose-equality) in modern codebases except intentional [null](concept:null-value) checks.
